---
name: gemini-summary
description: Use this skill when a user provides a web page URL, PDF file, video, or audio file and wants a Traditional Chinese summary. Prefer this skill for requests like "summarize this URL", "summarize this PDF", "summarize this video", "summarize this audio", "幫我摘要這篇", "這份報告重點是什麼", "幫我總結這段影片", "幫我摘要這段錄音", or when analyzing articles, documents, presentations, YouTube videos, audio recordings, or any content that needs a zh-TW summary. Automatically detects input type (web page, PDF, video, audio) — no manual type selection needed.
---

# 內容摘要 Skill

統一的摘要工具，自動偵測輸入類型（網頁 / PDF / 影片 / 音訊），使用 Gemini Interactions API 產出結構化的繁體中文（zh-TW）Markdown 摘要。

## 需求條件

- **GEMINI_API_KEY**：有效的 Google Gemini API 金鑰（設為環境變數）
- **Node.js** ≥ 20.0.0
- `scripts/summarize.js` 為預先建置的零依賴 bundle，不需 `npm install`

## 支援的輸入類型

| 類型 | 輸入格式 | 使用模型 |
|------|----------|----------|
| 網頁 | `https://example.com/article` | gemini-3-pro-preview |
| PDF | 本地 `./report.pdf` 或遠端 URL | gemini-3-pro-preview |
| 影片 | YouTube URL、影片 URL、本地影片檔、data URI | gemini-3.1-pro-preview |
| 音訊 | 本地音訊檔或遠端 URL | gemini-3-pro-preview |

### 自動偵測邏輯

- `data:` 開頭 → 影片
- HTTP/HTTPS URL：`.pdf` → PDF；影片副檔名或 YouTube 域名 → 影片；音訊副檔名 → 音訊；其他 → 網頁
- 本地檔案：`.pdf` → PDF；影片副檔名 → 影片；音訊副檔名 → 音訊
- 支援的影片格式：`.mp4`, `.mkv`, `.webm`, `.mov`, `.avi`, `.3gp`, `.m4v`, `.mpeg`, `.mpg`, `.ogv`
- 支援的音訊格式：`.mp3`, `.wav`, `.aac`, `.ogg`, `.flac`, `.m4a`, `.aiff`, `.wma`, `.opus`

## 使用方式

直接執行預建好的腳本，不需要先安裝依賴：

> ⚠️ **路徑安全**：skill 腳本位於 **repo 根目錄**的 `.agents/skills/` 下。若 cwd 不在 repo root，請先執行 `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` 取得絕對路徑，再 `cd` 到該路徑後執行。**禁止**在指令中使用 `$(...)` 語法（會被 Copilot CLI 安全過濾器擋下）。

```sh
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js <input>
```

### 範例

```sh
# 網頁摘要
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://example.com/posts/agentic-workflows"

# PDF 摘要（本地檔案）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "./reports/quarterly.pdf"

# PDF 摘要（遠端 URL）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://example.com/report.pdf"

# 影片摘要（YouTube）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 影片摘要（本地檔案）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "./clips/demo.mp4"

# 音訊摘要（本地檔案）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "./recordings/meeting.mp3"

# 音訊摘要（遠端 URL）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://example.com/podcast.mp3"
```

### 手動指定類型

自動偵測通常足夠，但可用 `--type` 覆蓋：

```sh
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js --type pdf "https://example.com/download?file=report"
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js --type audio "https://example.com/download?file=recording"
```

### Dry Run

設定 `SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1` 可在不呼叫 Gemini API 的情況下，預覽輸入偵測結果（JSON 格式）：

```sh
SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1 node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"
```

## 輸出格式

輸出為繁體中文 Markdown，包含以下區段：

```markdown
**📝 內容摘要**

**📌 來源**
- 類型：網頁 / PDF文件 / 影片 / 音訊
- 標題：{title}
- 網址：{url}

**💡 核心概述**
{請以流暢的段落整理脈絡，包含：主題背景、關鍵論點與結論}

**🔍 重點條列**
- {重點 1：包含具體的細節、範例或解釋}
- {重點 2：包含具體的細節、範例或解釋}
- ...

**📊 關鍵數據與事實**
（如有關鍵數字、金額、日期或事實才以條列呈現，若無則省略）
- 項目：數值/日期/事實

**🎯 行動建議**
{具體建議或「目前無明確行動建議」}
```

## Instructions for the Agent

⚠️ skill 腳本位於 **repo 根目錄**。若 cwd 不在 repo root，先獨立執行 `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` 取得路徑，再 `cd` 到該路徑後執行。禁止使用 `$(...)` 語法。

1. 確認使用者提供了 URL、PDF 檔案路徑、影片來源、或音訊檔案。
2. 確認環境中已設定 `GEMINI_API_KEY`。
3. 執行指令：
   ```sh
   node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "<input>"
   ```
4. 腳本會自動偵測輸入類型並選擇適當的處理方式與模型。
5. 結果以串流方式輸出到 stdout，進度與錯誤訊息輸出到 stderr。
6. 輸出中若包含連結，必須使用 Markdown 格式 `[文字](URL)`，不可使用 HTML `<a>` 標籤。
7. 如果 exit code 為 1，表示發生錯誤，請檢查 stderr 的錯誤訊息，不要自行編造摘要。
8. 可先用 `SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1` 測試輸入偵測是否正確。
9. 如果自動偵測不符預期，可用 `--type url|pdf|video|audio` 手動指定。

## 限制

### 網頁
- 只支援單一網址
- 不支援登入頁或高度依賴前端渲染的頁面
- 正文長度截斷至 120K 字元

### PDF
- 過大的 PDF（超過數十 MB）可能無法處理
- 掃描式 PDF 品質可能較低，建議使用有文字圖層的 PDF
- 無法處理加密 PDF

### 影片
- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 本地檔案會轉為 Base64 data URI，過大的檔案可能超出記憶體限制

### 音訊
- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 過大的音訊檔案可能超出 Gemini Files API 限制
- 支援格式：`.mp3`, `.wav`, `.aac`, `.ogg`, `.flac`, `.m4a`, `.aiff`, `.wma`, `.opus`

## 錯誤處理

| 錯誤訊息 | 說明 |
|---------|------|
| `缺少 GEMINI_API_KEY` | 未設定 API 金鑰環境變數 |
| `無法辨識輸入類型` | 輸入不是支援的 URL、PDF 或影片格式 |
| `fetch failed` | 網頁抓取失敗（網路或狀態碼問題） |
| `無法讀取檔案` | 本地檔案無法存取 |
| `下載失敗（HTTP xxx）` | 遠端 URL 回傳非 200 狀態碼 |
| `抓取逾時（30 秒）` | 遠端資源下載超過 30 秒 |

## 重建方式

`scripts/summarize.js` 是已提交的預建可執行產物；`src/summarize.js` 是可維護的原始碼。重建方式：

```sh
cd .agents/skills/gemini-summary
bun install
bun run build
```

> ⚠️ **路徑安全**：skill 腳本位於 **repo 根目錄**的 `.agents/skills/` 下。若 cwd 不在 repo root，請先執行 `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` 取得絕對路徑，再 `cd` 到該路徑後執行。**禁止**在指令中使用 `$(...)` 語法（會被 Copilot CLI 安全過濾器擋下）。

```sh
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js <input>
```

### 範例

```sh
# 網頁摘要
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://example.com/posts/agentic-workflows"

# PDF 摘要（本地檔案）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "./reports/quarterly.pdf"

# PDF 摘要（遠端 URL）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://example.com/report.pdf"

# 影片摘要（YouTube）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 影片摘要（本地檔案）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "./clips/demo.mp4"

# 音訊摘要（本地檔案）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "./recordings/meeting.mp3"

# 音訊摘要（遠端 URL）
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://example.com/podcast.mp3"
```

### 手動指定類型

自動偵測通常足夠，但可用 `--type` 覆蓋：

```sh
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js --type pdf "https://example.com/download?file=report"
node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js --type audio "https://example.com/download?file=recording"
```

### Dry Run

設定 `SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1` 可在不呼叫 Gemini API 的情況下，預覽輸入偵測結果（JSON 格式）：

```sh
SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1 node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"
```

## 輸出格式

輸出為繁體中文 Markdown，包含以下區段：

```markdown
**📝 內容摘要**

**📌 來源**
- 類型：網頁 / PDF文件 / 影片 / 音訊
- 標題：{title}
- 網址：{url}

**💡 核心概述**
{請以流暢的段落整理脈絡，包含：主題背景、關鍵論點與結論}

**🔍 重點條列**
- {重點 1：包含具體的細節、範例或解釋}
- {重點 2：包含具體的細節、範例或解釋}
- ...

**📊 關鍵數據與事實**
（如有關鍵數字、金額、日期或事實才以條列呈現，若無則省略）
- 項目：數值/日期/事實

**🎯 行動建議**
{具體建議或「目前無明確行動建議」}
```

## Instructions for the Agent

⚠️ skill 腳本位於 **repo 根目錄**。若 cwd 不在 repo root，先獨立執行 `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` 取得路徑，再 `cd` 到該路徑後執行。禁止使用 `$(...)` 語法。

1. 確認使用者提供了 URL、PDF 檔案路徑、影片來源、或音訊檔案。
2. 確認環境中已設定 `GEMINI_API_KEY`。
3. 執行指令：
   ```sh
   node .agents/skills/gemini-summary/scripts/summarize.js "<input>"
   ```
4. 腳本會自動偵測輸入類型並選擇適當的處理方式與模型。
5. 結果以串流方式輸出到 stdout，進度與錯誤訊息輸出到 stderr。
6. 輸出中若包含連結，必須使用 Markdown 格式 `[文字](URL)`，不可使用 HTML `<a>` 標籤。
7. 如果 exit code 為 1，表示發生錯誤，請檢查 stderr 的錯誤訊息，不要自行編造摘要。
8. 可先用 `SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1` 測試輸入偵測是否正確。
9. 如果自動偵測不符預期，可用 `--type url|pdf|video|audio` 手動指定。

## 限制

### 網頁
- 只支援單一網址
- 不支援登入頁或高度依賴前端渲染的頁面
- 正文長度截斷至 120K 字元

### PDF
- 過大的 PDF（超過數十 MB）可能無法處理
- 掃描式 PDF 品質可能較低，建議使用有文字圖層的 PDF
- 無法處理加密 PDF

### 影片
- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 本地檔案會轉為 Base64 data URI，過大的檔案可能超出記憶體限制

### 音訊
- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 過大的音訊檔案可能超出 Gemini Files API 限制
- 支援格式：`.mp3`, `.wav`, `.aac`, `.ogg`, `.flac`, `.m4a`, `.aiff`, `.wma`, `.opus`

## 錯誤處理

| 錯誤訊息 | 說明 |
|---------|------|
| `缺少 GEMINI_API_KEY` | 未設定 API 金鑰環境變數 |
| `無法辨識輸入類型` | 輸入不是支援的 URL、PDF 或影片格式 |
| `fetch failed` | 網頁抓取失敗（網路或狀態碼問題） |
| `無法讀取檔案` | 本地檔案無法存取 |
| `下載失敗（HTTP xxx）` | 遠端 URL 回傳非 200 狀態碼 |
| `抓取逾時（30 秒）` | 遠端資源下載超過 30 秒 |

## 重建方式

`scripts/summarize.js` 是已提交的預建可執行產物；`src/summarize.js` 是可維護的原始碼。重建方式：

```sh
cd .agents/skills/gemini-summary
bun install
bun run build
```

# 內容摘要 Skill

統一的摘要工具，自動偵測輸入類型（網頁 / PDF / 影片 / 音訊），使用 Gemini Interactions API 產出結構化的繁體中文（zh-TW）Markdown 摘要。

## 需求條件

- **GEMINI_API_KEY**：有效的 Google Gemini API 金鑰（設為環境變數）

| 影片 | YouTube URL、影片 URL、本地影片檔、data URI | gemini-3.1-pro-preview |

- 支援的音訊格式：`.mp3`, `.wav`, `.aac`, `.ogg`, `.flac`, `.m4a`, `.aiff`, `.wma`, `.opus`

4. 腳本會自動偵測輸入類型並選擇適當的處理方式與模型。
5. 結果以串流方式輸出到 stdout，進度與錯誤訊息輸出到 stderr。
6. 輸出中若包含連結，必須使用 Markdown 格式 `[文字](URL)`，不可使用 HTML `<a>` 標籤。
7. 如果 exit code 為 1，表示發生錯誤，請檢查 stderr 的錯誤訊息，不要自行編造摘要。
8. 可先用 `SUMMARY_DRY_RUN=1` 測試輸入偵測是否正確。
9. 如果自動偵測不符預期，可用 `--type url|pdf|video|audio` 手動指定。

## 限制

### 網頁
- 只支援單一網址
- 不支援登入頁或高度依賴前端渲染的頁面
- 正文長度截斷至 120K 字元

### PDF
- 過大的 PDF（超過數十 MB）可能無法處理
- 掃描式 PDF 品質可能較低，建議使用有文字圖層的 PDF
- 無法處理加密 PDF

### 影片
- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 本地檔案會轉為 Base64 data URI，過大的檔案可能超出記憶體限制

### 音訊
- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 過大的音訊檔案可能超出 Gemini Files API 限制
- 支援格式：`.mp3`, `.wav`, `.aac`, `.ogg`, `.flac`, `.m4a`, `.aiff`, `.wma`, `.opus`

## 錯誤處理

| 錯誤訊息 | 說明 |
|---------|------|
| `缺少 GEMINI_API_KEY` | 未設定 API 金鑰環境變數 |
| `無法辨識輸入類型` | 輸入不是支援的 URL、PDF 或影片格式 |
| `fetch failed` | 網頁抓取失敗（網路或狀態碼問題） |
| `無法讀取檔案` | 本地檔案無法存取 |
| `下載失敗（HTTP xxx）` | 遠端 URL 回傳非 200 狀態碼 |
| `抓取逾時（30 秒）` | 遠端資源下載超過 30 秒 |

## 重建方式

`scripts/summarize.js` 是已提交的預建可執行產物；`src/summarize.js` 是可維護的原始碼。重建方式：

```sh
cd .agents/skills/gemini-summary
bun install
bun run build
```

import re, json, urllib.request

print('TITLE MATCHES:')
for m in re.finditer(r'"title":\{"runs":\[\{"text":"(.*?)"\}\]\}', html):
    print(m.group(1)[:200])
    break
m=re.search(r'var ytInitialPlayerResponse = (\{.*?\});', html)
print('PLAYER', bool(m))
if m:
    print('player len', len(m.group(1)))

print('author:', obj.get('videoDetails',{}).get('author'))

cap=obj.get('captions',{}).get('playerCaptionsTracklistRenderer',{})
print('caption tracks:', len(cap.get('captionTracks',[])))

url='https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4'

api_key=re.search(r'INNERTUBE_API_KEY":"([^"]+)"', html).group(1)
name=re.search(r'INNERTUBE_CONTEXT_CLIENT_NAME":(\d+)', html).group(1)
ver=re.search(r'INNERTUBE_CONTEXT_CLIENT_VERSION":"([^"]+)"', html).group(1)
print('api_key', api_key[:10], 'client', name, ver)
body={
  'context': {'client': {'clientName': 'WEB','clientVersion': ver}},
  'videoId': 'PQU9o_5rHC4',
  'playbackContext': {'contentPlaybackContext': {'html5Preference': 'HTML5_PREF_WANTS'}}
}
req=urllib.request.Request('https://www.youtube.com/youtubei/v1/player?key='+api_key, data=json.dumps(body).encode(), headers={'Content-Type':'application/json','User-Agent':'Mozilla/5.0'})
resp=urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=30).read().decode('utf-8','ignore')
obj=json.loads(resp)
print('keys', obj.keys())
print('videoDetails keys', obj.get('videoDetails',{}).keys())
print('title', obj.get('videoDetails',{}).get('title'))

cap=obj.get('captions',{}).get('playerCaptionsTracklistRenderer',{})
print('tracks', len(cap.get('captionTracks',[])))

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
{"type":"text","text":{"value":"Here is a summary and key insights from “Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny”—drawn from an actual full transcript of the Y Combinator Light Cone podcast episode (Feb 17, 2026), as well as supporting resources and public discussions:

### Key Takeaways from the Conversation

**1. Building for the Future, Not the Present**
- Boris Cherny emphasizes that Claude Code was developed with a forward-looking approach: “We don't build for the model of today. We build for the model six months from now.” This mindset is critical in AI, as LLMs (large language models) rapidly evolve—features that seem futuristic now will become routine soon【3:0†source】.

**2. Iterative, User-Centric Development**
- The Claude Code tool has been “rewritten over and over and over.” There’s no code left from six months prior. The team constantly tries new things, releases to users, gathers feedback, and iterates. Sometimes these experiments don’t pan out, but the goal is to quickly converge on what works through frequent interaction with real users【3:0†source】【3:3†source】.

**3. Speed Over Bureaucracy**
- Cherny explains that the answer to engineering and operational errors—like the March 2026 accidental source code leak—is *more* automation and *more* speed, not more process. The human error that led to the leak (an internal debug artifact published by mistake) resulted in improvements to deployment automation, with even Claude itself contributing to future checks【3:2†source】【3:1†source】.

**4. Claude Code is Written By Claude**
- Remarkably, Boris Cherny claims he hasn't written any code by hand since November; all of Claude Code’s development is now performed by Claude itself, demonstrating the maturity and power of the system. The team spends its time orchestrating AI to write, run, and test code, rather than coding directly. This radical workflow is becoming the norm inside Anthropic and for a growing number of users externally【3:6†source】【3:3†source】【3:4†source】.

**5. Product Principles Driving Adoption**
- The product is intentionally “simple and general.” Instead of tightly scripting workflows, Anthropic provides a flexible development agent that allows users to invent new workflows on their own. Claude Code’s reach has expanded well beyond programmers—product managers, designers, and other knowledge workers use it regularly【3:6†source】.

**6. Real-World Impact and Tips**
- Claude Code now reportedly contributes to about 4% of all public GitHub commits. Power-user tips include: using `/clear` to avoid context pollution, maintaining `CLAUDE.md` for project-specific instructions, leveraging tab-completion, and thinking prompts like “think hard” or “ultrathink” to trigger deeper reasoning from the AI agent【3:5†source】【3:6†source】.

**7. Modern Team Culture**
- On management, Cherny highlights three guiding principles he gives to every new team member:
  - Underfund teams (to encourage scrappiness and creativity)
  - Give unlimited tokens (avoid unnecessary resource barriers for experiments)
  - Move fast, accept failure, and iterate quickly【3:3†source】.

### The Source Code Leak Story (March 2026)
- A developer error caused a complete source map of Claude Code—comprising over 500,000 lines of TypeScript, core LLM APIs, permission logic, and unreleased feature flags—to be published on npm. No model weights or user data were exposed. Cherny responded publicly, took responsibility, and detailed how the team improved processes to prevent future incidents【3:1†source】【3:2†source】.

### Practical Usage and Advice
- To maximize Claude Code, contributors and Cherny himself recommend:
  - Refreshing session context for accuracy
  - Maintaining explicit documentation that the agent can use
  - Taking advantage of productivity commands and intelligent interruption
  - Experimenting with new workflows, such as task handoff and context stacking【3:5†source】.

---

**Resources for Full Transcript, Notes, or Further Summaries:**

{"type":"text","text":{"value":"Here is a summary and key insights from “Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny”—drawn from an actual full transcript of the Y Combinator Light Cone podcast episode (Feb 17, 2026), as well as supporting resources and public discussions:

### Key Takeaways from the Conversation

**1. Building for the Future, Not the Present**
- Boris Cherny emphasizes that Claude Code was developed with a forward-looking approach: “We don't build for the model of today. We build for the model six months from now.” This mindset is critical in AI, as LLMs (large language models) rapidly evolve—features that seem futuristic now will become routine soon【3:0†source】.

**2. Iterative, User-Centric Development**
- The Claude Code tool has been “rewritten over and over and over.” There’s no code left from six months prior. The team constantly tries new things, releases to users, gathers feedback, and iterates. Sometimes these experiments don’t pan out, but the goal is to quickly converge on what works through frequent interaction with real users【3:0†source】【3:3†source】.

**3. Speed Over Bureaucracy**
- Cherny explains that the answer to engineering and operational errors—like the March 2026 accidental source code leak—is *more* automation and *more* speed, not more process. The human error that led to the leak (an internal debug artifact published by mistake) resulted in improvements to deployment automation, with even Claude itself contributing to future checks【3:2†source】【3:1†source】.

**4. Claude Code is Written By Claude**
- Remarkably, Boris Cherny claims he hasn't written any code by hand since November; all of Claude Code’s development is now performed by Claude itself, demonstrating the maturity and power of the system. The team spends its time orchestrating AI to write, run, and test code, rather than coding directly. This radical workflow is becoming the norm inside Anthropic and for a growing number of users externally【3:6†source】【3:3†source】【3:4†source】.

**5. Product Principles Driving Adoption**
- The product is intentionally “simple and general.” Instead of tightly scripting workflows, Anthropic provides a flexible development agent that allows users to invent new workflows on their own. Claude Code’s reach has expanded well beyond programmers—product managers, designers, and other knowledge workers use it regularly【3:6†source】.

**6. Real-World Impact and Tips**
- Claude Code now reportedly contributes to about 4% of all public GitHub commits. Power-user tips include: using `/clear` to avoid context pollution, maintaining `CLAUDE.md` for project-specific instructions, leveraging tab-completion, and thinking prompts like “think hard” or “ultrathink” to trigger deeper reasoning from the AI agent【3:5†source】【3:6†source】.

**7. Modern Team Culture**
- On management, Cherny highlights three guiding principles he gives to every new team member:
  - Underfund teams (to encourage scrappiness and creativity)
  - Give unlimited tokens (avoid unnecessary resource barriers for experiments)
  - Move fast, accept failure, and iterate quickly【3:3†source】.

### The Source Code Leak Story (March 2026)
- A developer error caused a complete source map of Claude Code—comprising over 500,000 lines of TypeScript, core LLM APIs, permission logic, and unreleased feature flags—to be published on npm. No model weights or user data were exposed. Cherny responded publicly, took responsibility, and detailed how the team improved processes to prevent future incidents【3:1†source】【3:2†source】.

### Practical Usage and Advice
- To maximize Claude Code, contributors and Cherny himself recommend:
  - Refreshing session context for accuracy
  - Maintaining explicit documentation that the agent can use
  - Taking advantage of productivity commands and intelligent interruption
  - Experimenting with new workflows, such as task handoff and context stacking【3:5†source】.

---

**Resources for Full Transcript, Notes, or Further Summaries:**
- [GitHub repo with full transcript (.md file)](https://github.com/njueeRay/claude-code-best-practice-ray/blob/dev/videos/claude-boris-y-combinator-17-feb-26.md)【3:0†source】
- [Podcast with summary and timestamps](https://podtail.com/en/podcast/lenny-s-podcast-product-growth-career/head-of-claude-code-what-happens-after-coding-is-s/)【3:3†source】
- [Complete Guide and Power User Article](https://www.theneuron.ai/explainer-articles/claude-code-guide-how-to-use-it%20/)【3:6†source】
- [Official developer productivity tips](https://zenn.dev/babushkai/articles/2026-01-20-claude-code-hidden-tips?locale=en)【3:5†source】

- Remarkably, Boris Cherny claims he hasn't written any code by hand since November; all of Claude Code’s development is now performed by Claude itself, demonstrating the maturity and power of the system. The team spends its time orchestrating AI to write, run, and test code, rather than coding directly. This radical workflow is becoming the norm inside Anthropic and for a growing number of users externally【3:6†source】【3:3†source】【3:4†source】.

**5. Product Principles Driving Adoption**
- The product is intentionally “simple and general.” Instead of tightly scripting workflows, Anthropic provides a flexible development agent that allows users to invent new workflows on their own. Claude Code’s reach has expanded well beyond programmers—product managers, designers, and other knowledge workers use it regularly【3:6†source】.

**6. Real-World Impact and Tips**
- Claude Code now reportedly contributes to about 4% of all public GitHub commits. Power-user tips include: using `/clear` to avoid context pollution, maintaining `CLAUDE.md` for project-specific instructions, leveraging tab-completion, and thinking prompts like “think hard” or “ultrathink” to trigger deeper reasoning from the AI agent【3:5†source】【3:6†source】.

**7. Modern Team Culture**
- On management, Cherny highlights three guiding principles he gives to every new team member:
  - Underfund teams (to encourage scrappiness and creativity)
  - Give unlimited tokens (avoid unnecessary resource barriers for experiments)
  - Move fast, accept failure, and iterate quickly【3:3†source】.

### The Source Code Leak Story (March 2026)
- A developer error caused a complete source map of Claude Code—comprising over 500,000 lines of TypeScript, core LLM APIs, permission logic, and unreleased feature flags—to be published on npm. No model weights or user data were exposed. Cherny responded publicly, took responsibility, and detailed how the team improved processes to prevent future incidents【3:1†source】【3:2†source】.

### Practical Usage and Advice
- To maximize Claude Code, contributors and Cherny himself recommend:
  - Refreshing session context for accuracy
  - Maintaining explicit documentation that the agent can use
  - Taking advantage of productivity commands and intelligent interruption
  - Experimenting with new workflows, such as task handoff and context stacking【3:5†source】.

---

successfully downloaded text file (SHA: 2776bc17e0d42ccb1597acfab32c6665dedac4d7)# Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny — Y Combinator Transcript of the interview with Boris Cherny ([@bcherny](https://x.com/bcherny)), creator of Claude Code, on the Y Combinator Light Cone podcast, published February 17, 2026.

--- ## Video Details - **Guest:** Boris Cherny (Creator of Claude Code) - **Host:** Y Combinator (The Light Cone) - **Published:** February 17, 2026 - **YouTube:** [Watch on YouTube](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4) --- ## Transcript [`0:01`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1) At Enthropic, the way that we thought about it is we don't build for the model of today. We build for the model six months from now. That's actually like still my advice to to founders that are building on LLM. Just try to think about like what is that frontier where the model is not very good at today cuz it's going to get good at it. All of Quad Code has just been written and rewritten and rewritten and rewritten over and over and over. There is no part of Quad Code that was around 6 months ago. You try a thing, you give it to users, you talk to users, you learn, and then eventually you might end up at a good idea. Sometimes you don't. Are you also in the back of your mind thinking that maybe like in 6 months you won't need to prompt that explicitly? Like the model will just be good enough to figure out on its own? [`0:36`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=36) Maybe in a month, [`0:38`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=38) no more need for plan mode in a month. Welcome to another episode of the light cone and today we have an extremely special guest, Boris Churnney, the creator engineer of Claude Code. Boris, thanks for joining us. [`0:59`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=59) Thanks for having me. [`44:28`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2668) because it was such like a silly prediction at the time. But really all it is is like you just like trace the you know the exponential [`44:34`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2674) and this is just like so deep in you know the DNA at cuz like you know three of our founders were co-authors of the scaling laws paper they kind of they saw this very early and so this is just like tracing the exponential this is what's going to happen and yes that happened. So continuing to trace the exponential I think what will happen is coding will be generally solved for everyone. Um, and I think today coding is practically solved, you know, for me and I think it'll be the case for everyone. Um, you know, regardless of domain, I think we're going to start to see the title software engineer go away. And I think it's just going to be maybe builder, maybe product manager, maybe we'll keep the title as kind of a vestigial thing, but the work that people do, it's not just going to be coding. It's software engineers are also going to be writing specs. They're going to be talking to users. like this thing that we're starting to see right now in our team where engineers are very much generalists and every single function on our team codes like our PM's code, our designers code, our EM codes, our um like everyone our our finance guy codes like everyone on our team codes. We're going to start to see this everywhere. So this is sort of uh this is kind of like the lower bound if we just continue the trend. The upper bound I think is a lot scarier. Um, and this is something like, you know, we hit ASL4. Um, and this, you know, at anthropic, we talked about these safety levels. ASL3 is where the models are right now. ASL4 is the model is recursively self-improving. Um, and so if this happens, essentially, we have to meet a bunch of criteria before we can release a model. And so the the extreme is that, you know, this happens um or there's some kind of catastrophic misuse like people are using the model to design bioiruses, design zero days, stuff like this. Um, and this is something that we're really really actively working on so that doesn't happen. I think uh it's just been honestly it's just been like so exciting and humbling like seeing how people are using quad code like uh you know I just wanted to build a cool thing and it ended up being really useful uh and that was so surprising and so exciting. [`46:23`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2783) My impression from Twitter or just the outside is basically everyone went away over the holidays and then like found out about Claude code and it's just been crazy ever since. Is that how it was for you at like internally? Did you were you having like a nice Christmas break and then came back and like what happened? Well, actually for all of December, I was traveling around. Uh, and I I took a coding vacation. So, we were kind of traveling around and I was just like coding every day. So, that was really nice. Uh, and then I also started to use Twitter at the time cuz like I I worked on Threads back then way back when. So, I've been a Threads user for a while. So, I just like tried to see kind of like other platforms where people are. Yeah. I think for a lot of people they kind of discovered that was the moment where they discovered Opus 4.5. I kind of already knew. [`47:01`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2821) Mhm. [`47:02`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2822) Uh, and internally quad code's just been on this like exponential tear for many many months now. So that just like it it became even more steep. That's what we saw. And if you look at cloud code now, you know, there was some stat from Mercury that like 70% of startups are you know choosing cloud as their model of choice. There was some other stat from like semi analysis that 4% of all public commits are made by cloud code. um like of all code written everywhere. All the companies, you know, use squad code from like the biggest companies to kind of, you know, smallest startups, you know, like it it wrote it it plotted the course for Perseverance like for like the Mars rover. This is just like this is the coolest thing for me. And we like we even printed posters cuz the team was like, "Wow, this is just like so cool that NASA chooses to use this thing." So, yeah, it's just like it's humbling. Um but it also feels like the very beginning. What's the sort of interaction between uh claude code and then co-work like you know was it a fork of cla code? Was it like you had cla code look at the cloud code and say let's make a new spec for nontechnical people that you know keeps all the lessons and then you know it sort of went off for a couple days and did that. What's the genesis of that and you know where do you think that goes? [`48:12`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2892) This is going to be like my fifth time using the word wait and demand. It was just that I mean like we we were looking at Twitter and there was like that one guy that was using quad code to like monitor his tomato plants. [`48:21`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2901) Mhm. [`48:23`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2903) Uh there was like this other person that was using it to like recover wedding photos off of a corrupted hard drive. There were people that using it for like uh for finance. When we looked internally at anthropic, every designer is using it all the entire finance team at this point is using it. The entire data science team is using it not for coding. People are jumping over hoops to install a thing in the terminal so that they could use this. So we knew for a while that we wanted to build something and so we're experimenting with a bunch of different ideas and the thing that kind of took off was just you know a little cloud code wrapper in a guey in the desktop app and that's all it is. It's just quad code under the hood. It's the same agent. [`48:55`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2935) Oh wow. [`48:58`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2938) Um and uh Felix and the team and Felix was early Electron contributor. He kind of knows that stack really well and he was hacking on various ideas and uh they they built it in I think something like 10 days. It was it was just like 100% written by quad code. Uh and it just felt ready to release. There was a lot of stuff that we had to build for nontechnical users. So it's a little bit different than a technical audience. Uh it runs in a all the code runs in a virtual machine. Uh there's a lot of delete uh protections for deletion and things like this. There's a lot of permission prompting and kind of other guardrails for users. Um yeah, it was honestly pretty obvious. Boris, thank you so much for making something that uh is taking away all my sleep, but in return, it's making me feel creator mode again, sort of founder mode again. It's been an exhilarating 3 weeks. I like can't believe I waited that long since November to actually get into it. Thank you so much for being with us. Thank you for building what you're building. [`49:54`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2994) Yeah, thanks for having me. And uh send bugs. [`49:59`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2999) Sounds good. --- ## Sources - [Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny — Y Combinator — YouTube](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4) - [Y Combinator](https://www.ycombinator.com/)

# Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny — Y Combinator Transcript of the interview with Boris Cherny ([@bcherny](https://x.com/bcherny)), creator of Claude Code, on the Y Combinator Light Cone podcast, published February 17, 2026.
← Back to Claude Code Best Practice
--- ## Video Details - **Guest:** Boris Cherny (Creator of Claude Code) - **Host:** Y Combinator (The Light Cone) - **Published:** February 17, 2026 - **YouTube:** [Watch on YouTube](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4) --- ## Transcript [`0:01`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1) At Enthropic, the way that we thought about it is we don't build for the model of today. We build for the model six months from now. That's actually like still my advice to to founders that are building on LLM. Just try to think about like what is that frontier where the model is not very good at today cuz it's going to get good at it. All of Quad Code has just been written and rewritten and rewritten and rewritten over and over and over. There is no part of Quad Code that was around 6 months ago. You try a thing, you give it to users, you talk to users, you learn, and then eventually you might end up at a good idea. Sometimes you don't. Are you also in the back of your mind thinking that maybe like in 6 months you won't need to prompt that explicitly? Like the model will just be good enough to figure out on its own? [`0:36`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=36) Maybe in a month, [`0:38`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=38) no more need for plan mode in a month. Welcome to another episode of the light cone and today we have an extremely special guest, Boris Churnney, the creator engineer of Claude Code. Boris, thanks for joining us. [`0:59`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=59) Thanks for having me. [`1:00`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=60) Thanks for creating a thing that has taken away my sleep for about 3 weeks straight. [`1:07`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=67) I am very addicted to Cloud Code and uh it feels like rocket boosters. Has it felt like this for people like for you know months at this point. I think it was like end of November is where uh a lot of my friends said like something changed. [`1:21`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=81) I remember for me I felt this way when I first created Quad Code and I didn't yet know if I was on to something. I kind of felt like I was on to something and then that's when I wasn't sleeping. [`1:28`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=88) Yeah. [`1:29`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=89) And that was just like three straight months. [`1:33`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=93) This was uh September 2024. Yeah. It was like three straight months. I I didn't take a single day vacation. Worked through the weekends. Worked every single night. I was just like, "Oh my god, this is I think this is going to be a thing. I don't know if it's useful yet because it it couldn't actually code yet." [`1:48`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=108) If you look back on uh those moments to now, like what would be like the most surprising thing about this moment right now? [`1:54`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=114) It's unbelievable that we're still using a terminal. That was supposed to be the starting point. I didn't think that would be the ending point. And then the second one is that it's even useful cuz uh you know at the beginning it didn't really write code. Even in February when we G it wrote maybe like 10% of my code or something like that. I didn't really use it to write code. it wasn't very good at it. I still wrote most of my code by hand. Uh so the fact that it it actually like our bets paid off and it got good at the thing that we thought it was going to get good at because it wasn't obvious. At Enthropic, the way that we thought about it is we don't build for the model of today. We build for the model 6 months from now. And that's actually like still my advice to to founders that are building on LLM is, you know, just try to think about like what is that frontier where the model is not very good at today. um because it's going to get good at it and you just have to wait. [`2:39`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=159) Going back though, but when do you remember when you first got the idea? Can you just talk us through that? Like was it some like a spark or what was even the first version of it in your mind? [`2:47`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=167) You know, it's funny. It was like it was so accidental that it just kind of evolved into this. Um you know as as anthropic I think for Ant the bet has been coding for a long time and the bet has been the path to save to safe AGI is through coding [`3:03`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=183) and this is this has kind of always been the idea and the way you get there is you you teach the model how to code then you teach it how to use tools then you teach it how to use computers um and you can kind of see that because the the first team that I joined at Enthropic it was called the anthropic labs team uh and it produced three products it was quadcode MCP and in the desktop app. So you can kind of see how these like weave together. The particular product that we built, you know, like no one no one asked me to build a CLI. Um we kind of knew maybe it was time to build some kind of coding product cuz it seemed like the model was ready, but no one had yet really built the product that harnessed this capability. So like still there's this insane feeling of product overhang. But at the time it was just like even crazier cuz like no one had built this yet. And so I I started like hacking around uh and I was like, "Okay, we build a coding product. What do I have to do first? I have to understand how to use the API because I hadn't used anthropic API at that point." Um and so I I just built like a little terminal app to use the API. That's all that I did. And it was a little chat app because you know like you think about the you know AI applications of the time and you know for non-coders today most what what are most people using is just a chat app. So that's what I built. Uh and you know it was in a terminal. I can ask questions. I give answers. Then I think tool use came out. I just wanted to try out tool use because I I don't really understand what this is. I was like to use this is cool. Is this actually useful? Probably not. Let me just try it. [`4:25`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=265) You built it in terminal just because it was the easiest way to get something up and running. [`4:29`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=269) Yes. Cuz I didn't have to build a UI. [`4:30`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=270) Okay. [`4:31`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=271) It was just me [`4:32`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=272) at that point. It was like the IDEs, Cursor, Windsurf taking off. We build for the model six months from now. That's actually like still my advice to to founders that are building on LLM. Just try to think about like what is that frontier where the model is not very good at today cuz it's going to get good at it. All of Quad Code has just been written and rewritten and rewritten and rewritten over and over and over. There is no part of Quad Code that was around 6 months ago. You try a thing, you give it to users, you talk to users, you learn, and then eventually you might end up at a good idea. Sometimes you don't. Are you also in the back of your mind thinking that maybe like in 6 months you won't need to prompt that explicitly? Like the model will just be good enough to figure out on its own? [`0:36`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=36) Maybe in a month, [`0:38`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=38) no more need for plan mode in a month. Welcome to another episode of the light cone and today we have an extremely special guest, Boris Churnney, the creator engineer of Claude Code. Boris, thanks for joining us. [`0:59`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=59) Thanks for having me. [`44:28`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2668) because it was such like a silly prediction at the time. But really all it is is like you just like trace the you know the exponential [`44:34`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2674) and this is just like so deep in you know the DNA at cuz like you know three of our founders were co-authors of the scaling laws paper they kind of they saw this very early and so this is just like tracing the exponential this is what's going to happen and yes that happened. So continuing to trace the exponential I think what will happen is coding will be generally solved for everyone. Um, and I think today coding is practically solved, you know, for me and I think it'll be the case for everyone. Um, you know, regardless of domain, I think we're going to start to see the title software engineer go away. And I think it's just going to be maybe builder, maybe product manager, maybe we'll keep the title as kind of a vestigial thing, but the work that people do, it's not just going to be coding. It's software engineers are also going to be writing specs. They're going to be talking to users. like this thing that we're starting to see right now in our team where engineers are very much generalists and every single function on our team codes like our PM's code, our designers code, our EM codes, our um like everyone our our finance guy codes like everyone on our team codes. We're going to start to see this everywhere. So this is sort of uh this is kind of like the lower bound if we just continue the trend. The upper bound I think is a lot scarier. Um, and this is something like, you know, we hit ASL4. Um, and this, you know, at anthropic, we talked about these safety levels. ASL3 is where the models are right now. toolResult.textResultForLlm:
25: [`0:01`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1) At Enthropic, the way that we thought about it is we don't build for the model of today. We build for the model six months from now. That's actually like still my advice to to founders that are building on LLM. Just try to think about like what is that frontier where the model is not very good at today cuz it's going to get good at it. All of Quad Code has just been written and rewritten and rewritten and rewritten over and over and over. There is no part of Quad Code that was around 6 months ago. You try a thing, you give it to users, you talk to users, you learn, and then eventually you might end up at a good idea. Sometimes you don't. Are you also in the back of your mind thinking that maybe like in 6 months you won't need to prompt that explicitly? Like the model will just be good enough to figure out on its own?
119: [`14:58`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=898) And I'm just constantly surprised by this. Like for example, there was a we had like a memory leak and we were trying to debug it. Um and by the way, like Jared Sumar has just been on this crusade killing all the memory leaks and it's just been amazing. But before Jared was on the team, I had to do this and there was this memory leak. I I was trying to debug it. And so I I took a heap dump. I opened it in DevTools. I was looking through the profile. Then I was looking through the code and I I was just trying to figure this out. And then another engineer on the team, Chris, he just like asked Quad Code. He was like, "Hey, I think there's a memory leak. Can you like run this?" And then like try to figure it out. And Quad Code like took the heap dump. It wrote a little tool for itself to like analyze the heap dump. And then it found the leak faster than I did. And this is just something I have to constantly relearn because my brain is still stuck somewhere six months ago at times.
139: [`20:42`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1242) I guess one of the uh funnier things that I've been having office hours with various founders about is um you have like sort of the visionary founder who has like the idea they've like built this like crystal palace of the product that they want to build. they've totally loaded in their brain, you know, who the user is and what they feel and what they're motivated by and then they're sitting in claude code and they can do like, you know, 50x work and then but they have engineers who work for them who like don't have the, you know, crystal memory palace of like the platonic ideal of the product that the pro founder has and they can only do like 5x work. Are you hearing stories like that? there's usually a person who's like the core like designer of a thing and they're just like, you know, trying to blast it out of their brain. What's the nature of like teams like that? You know, it seems like that's almost a stable configuration. Like you're going to have the visionary who like now is unleashed, but you know, maybe going back to the top of it, like I'm experiencing this right now. I was like, "Oh, well, I'm only a solo person and you know, I need to eat and sleep and I have, you know, a whole job. It's like, how am I going to do this?" You know,
153: [`23:25`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1405) Right. If you if you think about the way that uh you know like how our agents actually started nowadays and you know I haven't pulled the data on this but I would bet the majority of agents are actually prompted by quad today in the form of uh sub agents cuz like a sub agent is just like a recursive quad code that's all it is in the code and it's just prompted by we call her mama quad
165: [`24:44`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1484) It's kind of case by case, you know, like quadm like what is it? It's just a it's a shortcut. Like if you find yourself repeating the same thing over and over, you put in the quad MD. But otherwise, you don't have to put everything there. You can just prompt quad.
167: [`24:56`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1496) Are you also in the back of your mind thinking that maybe like in six months, you won't need to prompt that explicitly? Like the model will just be good enough to figure out on its own.
179: [`25:12`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1512) That's some alpha for everyone here. What would the world look like without plan mode? Do you just describe it at the prompt level and it would just do it? One shot it? Yeah, we've uh we've started experimenting with this because quad code can now enter plan mode by itself. I don't know if you've you guys have seen that.
183: [`25:28`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1528) So, we're trying to kind of get this experience really good. So, it would enter plan mode the same point where a human would have wanted to enter it. So, I think it's like I think it's something like this, but actually plan mode there's no there's no big secret to it. All it does is it adds one sentence to the prompt that's like please don't code.
193: [`26:49`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=1609) I kind of think about it in terms of like kind of increasing model capabilities. So maybe 6 months ago a plan was insufficient. So you get Claude to make a plan. Let's say even with plan mode you still have to kind of sit there and babysit cuz it can go off track. Nowadays what I do is probably 80% of my sessions I say I say plan mode has a limited lifespan but I I'm a heavy plan mode user. Um I probably 80% of my sessions I start in plan mode and claude will you know it'll start it'll start making a plan. I'll move on to my second terminal tab and then I'll have it make another plan and then when I run out of tabs I open the desktop app and then I go to the code tab and then I just start a bunch of tabs there and they all start in plan mode probably know like 80% of the time. Once the plan is good, and sometimes it takes a little back and forth, they just get clawed to execute. And nowadays, what I find with Opus 4.5, I think it started with 4.6 it got really good. Once the plan is good, it just stays on track and it'll just do the thing exactly right almost every time. And so, you know, before you had to babysit after the plan and before the plan, now it's just before the plan. So, maybe the next thing is you just won't have to babysit. You can just kind of give a prompt and Quad will figure it out.
329: [`48:58`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2938) Um and uh Felix and the team and Felix was early Electron contributor. He kind of knows that stack really well and he was hacking on various ideas and uh they they built it in I think something like 10 days. It was it was just like 100% written by quad code. Uh and it just felt ready to release. There was a lot of stuff that we had to build for nontechnical users. So it's a little bit different than a technical audience. Uh it runs in a all the code runs in a virtual machine. Uh there's a lot of delete uh protections for deletion and things like this. There's a lot of permission prompting and kind of other guardrails for users. Um yeah, it was honestly pretty obvious. Boris, thank you so much for making something that uh is taking away all my sleep, but in return, it's making me feel creator mode again, sort of founder mode again. It's been an exhilarating 3 weeks. I like can't believe I waited that long since November to actually get into it. Thank you so much for being with us. Thank you for building what you're building.
<exited with exit code 0>

Was it like you had cla code look at the cloud code and say let's make a new spec for nontechnical people that you know keeps all the lessons and then you know it sort of went off for a couple days and did that. What's the genesis of that and you know where do you think that goes?
 
 [`48:12`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2892) This is going to be like my fifth time using the word wait and demand. It was just that I mean like we we were looking at Twitter and there was like that one guy that was using quad code to like monitor his tomato plants.
 
 [`48:21`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2901) Mhm.
 
 [`48:23`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2903) Uh there was like this other person that was using it to like recover wedding photos off of a corrupted hard drive. There were people that using it for like uh for finance. When we looked internally at anthropic, every designer is using it all the entire finance team at this point is using it. The entire data science team is using it not for coding. People are jumping over hoops to install a thing in the terminal so that they could use this. So we knew for a while that we wanted to build something and so we're experimenting with a bunch of different ideas and the thing that kind of took off was just you know a little cloud code wrapper in a guey in the desktop app and that's all it is. It's just quad code under the hood. It's the same agent.
 
 [`48:55`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2935) Oh wow.
 
 [`48:58`](https://youtu.be/PQU9o_5rHC4?t=2938) Um and uh Felix and the team and Felix was early Electron contributor. He kind of knows that stack really well and he was hacking on various ideas and uh they they built it in I think something like 10 days. It was it was just like 100% written by quad code. Uh and it just felt ready to release. There was a lot of stuff that we had to build for nontechnical users. So it's a little bit different than a technical audience. Uh it runs in a all the code runs in a virtual machine. Uh there's a lot of delete uh protections for deletion and things like this. There's a lot of permission prompting and kind of other guardrails for users. Um yeah, it was honestly pretty obvious. Boris, thank you so much for making something that uh is taking away all my sleep, but in return, it's making me feel creator mode again, sort of founder mode again. It's been an exhilarating 3 weeks. I like can't believe I waited that long since November to actually get into it. Thank you so much for being with us. Thank you for building what you're building.

toolRequests.name: apply_patch
toolRequests.arguments: *** Begin Patch *** Add File: /home/runner/work/little-hou-claw/little-hou-claw/artifacts/4191189220/result.md +# 內容摘要 + +**📌 來源** +- 類型：影片 +- 標題：Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny +- 網址：https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4 + +**💡 核心概述** +這支訪談主要在談 Claude Code 為什麼會成功：核心不是把現有 IDE 做得更漂亮，而是持續追著模型能力往前走，並把產品設計成能跟著模型一起演化。Boris Cherny 強調，產品要以「六個月後的模型」來設計，而不是今天的模型；因此 Claude Code 會反覆重寫、快速試驗、從使用者回饋中迭代，甚至連 plan mode、sub-agents、CLAUDE.md 這些功能，都是從真實使用情境裡長出來的。 + +訪談也提到，隨著模型變強，開發者的工作型態正在改變：從手動寫碼，轉向寫規格、帶著代理做計畫、檢查結果與修正偏差。Anthropic 內部已經有許多工程、設計、PM、財務等角色都在用 Claude Code 工作，顯示這不只是編輯器工具，而是新的軟體生產方式。 + +**🔍 重點條列** +- Claude Code 的設計哲學是「不要為今天的模型設計」，而要為未來幾個月內會變強的模型設計。 +- 產品大量依賴快速迭代：功能會一直被重寫、工具會持續更換，沒有什麼部分能維持半年不變。 +- CLAUDE.md 的定位很克制：只放重複出現、真的有幫助的規則，太長反而該刪掉重來。 +- plan mode 只是把「請先不要寫碼」這個意圖加進提示詞，但在實務上能大幅改善複雜任務的穩定度。 +- sub-agents 被用來做平行除錯、查 log、查 code path，讓模型自己把工作拆開。 +- 產品不是只服務工程師；Anthropic 內部很多非工程角色也在用同一套工具，因為它本質上是通用代理。 +- Boris 認為未來的工作重心會從「寫程式」轉向「寫規格、跟使用者對話、指揮代理執行」。 + +**📊 關鍵數據與事實** +- 6 個月：產品設計時看的時間尺度。 +- 約 80%：他自己多數 session 會先從 plan mode 開始。 +- 約 150%：Claude Code 出現後，Anthropic 每位工程師產能成長幅度。 +- 約 70%～90%：Anthropic 內部不同團隊的程式碼由 Claude 產出比例。 +- 約 4%：公開 GitHub commits 來自 Claude Code 的引用數據。 + +**🎯 行動建議** +- 做 AI 工具時，先假設模型很快會變強，避免把產品綁死在今天的能力上。 +- 把常見錯誤收進可重用的規則檔，但保持精簡，避免累積成負擔。 +- 複雜任務先讓模型規劃，再讓它執行；必要時用多個 sub-agents 平行探索。 +- 把重點放在使用者回饋與快速迭代，而不是一次把流程設計到完美。 *** End Patch

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. # 內容摘要
2. 
3. **📌 來源**
4. - 類型：影片
5. - 標題：Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny
6. - 網址：[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4)
7. 
8. **💡 核心概述**
9. 這支訪談主要在談 Claude Code 為什麼會成功：核心不是把現有 IDE 做得更漂亮，而是持續追著模型能力往前走，並把產品設計成能跟著模型一起演化。Boris Cherny 強調，產品要以「六個月後的模型」來設計，而不是今天的模型；因此 Claude Code 會反覆重寫、快速試驗、從使用者回饋中迭代，甚至連 plan mode、sub-agents、CLAUDE.md 這些功能，都是從真實使用情境裡長出來的。
10. 
11. 訪談也提到，隨著模型變強，開發者的工作型態正在改變：從手動寫碼，轉向寫規格、帶著代理做計畫、檢查結果與修正偏差。Anthropic 內部已經有許多工程、設計、PM、財務等角色都在用 Claude Code 工作，顯示這不只是編輯器工具，而是新的軟體生產方式。
12. 
13. **🔍 重點條列**
14. - Claude Code 的設計哲學是「不要為今天的模型設計」，而要為未來幾個月內會變強的模型設計。
15. - 產品大量依賴快速迭代：功能會一直被重寫、工具會持續更換，沒有什麼部分能維持半年不變。
16. - CLAUDE.md 的定位很克制：只放重複出現、真的有幫助的規則，太長反而該刪掉重來。
17. - plan mode 只是把「請先不要寫碼」這個意圖加進提示詞，但在實務上能大幅改善複雜任務的穩定度。
18. - sub-agents 被用來做平行除錯、查 log、查 code path，讓模型自己把工作拆開。
19. - 產品不是只服務工程師；Anthropic 內部很多非工程角色也在用同一套工具，因為它本質上是通用代理。
20. - Boris 認為未來的工作重心會從「寫程式」轉向「寫規格、跟使用者對話、指揮代理執行」。
21. 
22. **📊 關鍵數據與事實**
23. - 6 個月：產品設計時看的時間尺度。
24. - 約 80%：他自己多數 session 會先從 plan mode 開始。
25. - 約 150%：Claude Code 出現後，Anthropic 每位工程師產能成長幅度。
26. - 約 70%～90%：Anthropic 內部不同團隊的程式碼由 Claude 產出比例。
27. - 約 4%：公開 GitHub commits 來自 Claude Code 的引用數據。
28. 
29. **🎯 行動建議**
30. - 做 AI 工具時，先假設模型很快會變強，避免把產品綁死在今天的能力上。
31. - 把常見錯誤收進可重用的規則檔，但保持精簡，避免累積成負擔。
32. - 複雜任務先讓模型規劃，再讓它執行；必要時用多個 sub-agents 平行探索。
33. - 把重點放在使用者回饋與快速迭代，而不是一次把流程設計到完美。
34.

 # 內容摘要
 
 **📌 來源**
 - 類型：影片
 - 標題：Inside Claude Code With Its Creator Boris Cherny
 - 網址：[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4)
 
 **💡 核心概述**
 這支訪談主要在談 Claude Code 為什麼會成功：核心不是把現有 IDE 做得更漂亮，而是持續追著模型能力往前走，並把產品設計成能跟著模型一起演化。Boris Cherny 強調，產品要以「六個月後的模型」來設計，而不是今天的模型；因此 Claude Code 會反覆重寫、快速試驗、從使用者回饋中迭代，甚至連 plan mode、sub-agents、CLAUDE.md 這些功能，都是從真實使用情境裡長出來的。
 
 訪談也提到，隨著模型變強，開發者的工作型態正在改變：從手動寫碼，轉向寫規格、帶著代理做計畫、檢查結果與修正偏差。Anthropic 內部已經有許多工程、設計、PM、財務等角色都在用 Claude Code 工作，顯示這不只是編輯器工具，而是新的軟體生產方式。
 
 **🔍 重點條列**
 - Claude Code 的設計哲學是「不要為今天的模型設計」，而要為未來幾個月內會變強的模型設計。
 - 產品大量依賴快速迭代：功能會一直被重寫、工具會持續更換，沒有什麼部分能維持半年不變。
 - CLAUDE.md 的定位很克制：只放重複出現、真的有幫助的規則，太長反而該刪掉重來。
 - plan mode 只是把「請先不要寫碼」這個意圖加進提示詞，但在實務上能大幅改善複雜任務的穩定度。
 - sub-agents 被用來做平行除錯、查 log、查 code path，讓模型自己把工作拆開。
 - 產品不是只服務工程師；Anthropic 內部很多非工程角色也在用同一套工具，因為它本質上是通用代理。
 - Boris 認為未來的工作重心會從「寫程式」轉向「寫規格、跟使用者對話、指揮代理執行」。
 
 **📊 關鍵數據與事實**
 - 6 個月：產品設計時看的時間尺度。
 - 約 80%：他自己多數 session 會先從 plan mode 開始。
 - 約 150%：Claude Code 出現後，Anthropic 每位工程師產能成長幅度。
 - 約 70%～90%：Anthropic 內部不同團隊的程式碼由 Claude 產出比例。
 - 約 4%：公開 GitHub commits 來自 Claude Code 的引用數據。
 
 **🎯 行動建議**
 - 做 AI 工具時，先假設模型很快會變強，避免把產品綁死在今天的能力上。
 - 把常見錯誤收進可重用的規則檔，但保持精簡，避免累積成負擔。
 - 複雜任務先讓模型規劃，再讓它執行；必要時用多個 sub-agents 平行探索。
 - 把重點放在使用者回饋與快速迭代，而不是一次把流程設計到完美。

toolResult.sessionLog:
Skill loaded successfully ✅

---
name: gemini-subtitle
description: Use this skill when a user provides a YouTube URL, video URL, or video file and wants Traditional Chinese subtitles (SRT or VTT format). Prefer this skill for requests like "幫影片加中文字幕", "生成繁體中文字幕", "add Chinese subtitles", "幫我把這個 YouTube 影片加字幕", or any request to generate zh-TW subtitles for a video.
---

# 繁體中文字幕生成 Skill

為 YouTube 影片、遠端影片 URL 或本地影片檔產生繁體中文（zh-TW）字幕，輸出為標準 SRT 或 WebVTT 格式。

## 需求條件

- **GEMINI_API_KEY**：有效的 Google Gemini API 金鑰（設為環境變數）
- **Node.js** ≥ 20.0.0
- `scripts/subtitle.js` 為預先建置的零依賴 bundle，不需 `npm install`

## 支援的輸入類型

| 類型 | 輸入格式 |
|------|----------|
| YouTube 影片 | `https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xxx` 或 `https://youtu.be/xxx` |
| 遠端影片 URL | `https://example.com/video.mp4` |
| 本地影片檔 | `./clips/demo.mp4`（支援 .mp4, .mkv, .webm, .mov, .avi 等） |

### 支援的影片格式
`.mp4`, `.mkv`, `.webm`, `.mov`, `.avi`, `.3gp`, `.m4v`, `.mpeg`, `.mpg`, `.ogv`

## 使用方式

```sh
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js <input>
```

```sh
# YouTube 字幕
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=abc123"

# YouTube 短網址
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 遠端影片 URL
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://example.com/video.mp4"

# 本地影片檔
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "./clips/demo.mp4"

# 儲存為 SRT 檔案
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js --output subtitles.srt "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 輸出 WebVTT 格式
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js --format vtt "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 輸出 WebVTT 並儲存
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js --format vtt --output subtitles.vtt "https://youtu.be/abc123"
```

### Dry Run

設定 `SUBTITLE_DRY_RUN=1` 可在不呼叫 Gemini API 的情況下，預覽輸入偵測結果：


```
1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,500
```

設定 `SUBTITLE_DRY_RUN=1` 可在不呼叫 Gemini API 的情況下，預覽輸入偵測結果：

```sh
SUBTITLE_DRY_RUN=1 node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"
```

## 輸出格式

### SRT 格式（預設）

```
1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,500
歡迎來到今天的節目。

2
00:00:04,800 --> 00:00:08,200
今天我們將探討人工智慧的最新發展。
```

### WebVTT 格式（`--format vtt`）

```
WEBVTT

00:00:01.000 --> 00:00:04.500
歡迎來到今天的節目。

00:00:04.800 --> 00:00:08.200
今天我們將探討人工智慧的最新發展。
```

## Instructions for the Agent

⚠️ skill 腳本位於 **repo 根目錄**。若 cwd 不在 repo root，先獨立執行 `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` 取得路徑，再 `cd` 到該路徑後執行。禁止使用 `$(...)` 語法。

1. 確認使用者提供了 YouTube URL、影片 URL 或本地影片檔案路徑。
2. 確認環境中已設定 `GEMINI_API_KEY`。
3. 決定輸出格式（預設 SRT，若使用者要求 WebVTT 則加 `--format vtt`）。
4. 決定是否需要儲存到檔案（若使用者要求儲存，則加 `--output <filename>`）。
5. 執行指令：
   ```sh
   node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "<input>"
   ```
6. 字幕內容以串流方式輸出到 stdout。進度與錯誤訊息輸出到 stderr。
7. 如果 exit code 為 1，表示發生錯誤，請檢查 stderr 的錯誤訊息，不要自行編造字幕。
8. 可先用 `SUBTITLE_DRY_RUN=1` 測試輸入偵測是否正確。
9. 若使用者要求儲存字幕檔案，務必加上 `--output <filename>` 參數。
10. 輸出字幕時，直接呈現 SRT/VTT 內容，不要加上任何額外說明文字。

## 限制

- 遠端 URL 有 30 秒逾時限制
- 本地檔案會轉為 Base64 data URI，過大的檔案可能超出記憶體限制
- 字幕時間精確度取決於 Gemini 模型的影片理解能力
- 不支援僅有背景音樂而無語音的影片

## 錯誤處理

| 錯誤訊息 | 說明 |
|---------|------|
| `缺少 GEMINI_API_KEY` | 未設定 API 金鑰環境變數 |
| `無法辨識輸入類型` | 輸入不是支援的 URL 或影片格式 |
| `找不到影片檔案` | 本地檔案路徑不存在 |
| `抓取逾時（30 秒）` | 遠端資源下載超過 30 秒 |

## 重建方式

`scripts/subtitle.js` 是已提交的預建可執行產物；`src/subtitle.js` 是可維護的原始碼。重建方式：

```sh
cd .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle
bun install
bun run build
```

data.name: gemini-subtitle
data.content:
---
name: gemini-subtitle
description: Use this skill when a user provides a YouTube URL, video URL, or video file and wants Traditional Chinese subtitles (SRT or VTT format). Prefer this skill for requests like "幫影片加中文字幕", "生成繁體中文字幕", "add Chinese subtitles", "幫我把這個 YouTube 影片加字幕", or any request to generate zh-TW subtitles for a video.
---

# 繁體中文字幕生成 Skill

為 YouTube 影片、遠端影片 URL 或本地影片檔產生繁體中文（zh-TW）字幕，輸出為標準 SRT 或 WebVTT 格式。

## 需求條件

- **GEMINI_API_KEY**：有效的 Google Gemini API 金鑰（設為環境變數）
- **Node.js** ≥ 20.0.0
- `scripts/subtitle.js` 為預先建置的零依賴 bundle，不需 `npm install`

## 支援的輸入類型

| 類型 | 輸入格式 |
|------|----------|
| YouTube 影片 | `https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xxx` 或 `https://youtu.be/xxx` |
| 遠端影片 URL | `https://example.com/video.mp4` |
| 本地影片檔 | `./clips/demo.mp4`（支援 .mp4, .mkv, .webm, .mov, .avi 等） |

### 支援的影片格式
`.mp4`, `.mkv`, `.webm`, `.mov`, `.avi`, `.3gp`, `.m4v`, `.mpeg`, `.mpg`, `.ogv`

## 使用方式

直接執行預建好的腳本，不需要先安裝依賴：

> ⚠️ **路徑安全**：skill 腳本位於 **repo 根目錄**的 `.agents/skills/` 下。若 cwd 不在 repo root，請先執行 `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` 取得絕對路徑，再 `cd` 到該路徑後執行。**禁止**在指令中使用 `$(...)` 語法。

```sh
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js <input>
```

### 範例

```sh
# YouTube 字幕
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=abc123"

# YouTube 短網址
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 遠端影片 URL
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://example.com/video.mp4"

# 本地影片檔
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "./clips/demo.mp4"

# 儲存為 SRT 檔案
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js --output subtitles.srt "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 輸出 WebVTT 格式
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js --format vtt "https://youtu.be/abc123"

# 輸出 WebVTT 並儲存
node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js --format vtt --output subtitles.vtt "https://youtu.be/abc123"
```

### Dry Run

設定 `SUBTITLE_DRY_RUN=1` 可在不呼叫 Gemini API 的情況下，預覽輸入偵測結果：

```sh
SUBTITLE_DRY_RUN=1 node .agents/skills/gemini-subtitle/scripts/subtitle.js "https://youtu.be/abc123"
```

## 輸出格式

### SRT 格式（預設）

```
1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:04,500
歡迎來到今天的節目。

2
00:00:04,800 --> 00:00:08,200
今天我們將探討人工智慧的最新發展。
```

### WebVTT 格式（`--format vtt`）

```
WEBVTT

00:00:01.000 --> 00:00:04.500
歡迎來到今天的節目。

00:00:04.800 --> 00:00:08.200
今天我們將探討人工智慧的最新發展。
```

3. 決定輸出格式（預設 SRT，若使用者要求 WebVTT 則加 `--format vtt`）。
4. 決定是否需要儲存到檔案（若使用者要求儲存，則加 `--output <filename>`）。
5. 執行指令：

在 Anthropic，我們的思考方式是
我們不為今天的模型進行開發

2

而是為六個月後的模型做準備

3
00:00:06,440 --> 00:00:10,240
這其實仍然是我對
那些基於 LLM 開發的創辦人們的建議

4
00:00:10,240 --> 00:00:14,640
試著去思考，目前模型
還不太擅長的領域邊界在哪裡？

00:00:14,640 --> 00:00:18,520
因為它終將會變得擅長
Claude Code 的所有程式碼

6
00:00:18,520 --> 00:00:21,480
都經過了一次又一次的不斷重寫

7
00:00:21,480 --> 00:00:27,160
Claude Code 現在沒有任何部分
是六個月前留下來的

8
00:00:27,160 --> 00:00:31,040
你嘗試新事物、交給用戶
與用戶交流、並從中學習

9
00:00:31,040 --> 00:00:34,360
最終你可能會得到一個好點子

00:00:34,360 --> 00:00:38,440

模型本身就足以聰明到能自行理解？

Boris：天啊。

00:00:50,000 --> 00:01:00,000
[LIGHTCONE PODCAST]

15
00:01:00,000 --> 00:01:03,000
歡迎收看新一集的 Lightcone
今天我們有一位非常特別的嘉賓

16
00:01:03,000 --> 00:01:07,000
Boris Cherny，他是

17
00:01:07,000 --> 00:01:10,000
Boris，感謝你的加入
謝謝你們邀請我

18
00:01:10,000 --> 00:01:13,760
感謝你創造了一個讓我連續
三週睡不著覺的東西

00:01:13,760 --> 00:01:18,240
我對 Claude Code 非常著迷
它感覺就像是火箭助推器

00:01:18,240 --> 00:01:21,720
人們是否也有同感？
大約在去年 11 月底

21
00:01:21,720 --> 00:01:25,320
我很多朋友都說，有些東西改變了

22
00:01:25,320 --> 00:01:28,320

當我剛創造出 Claude Code 時

23
00:01:28,320 --> 00:01:31,680
雖然還不確定是否能成功

24
00:01:31,680 --> 00:01:34,840
那時我也開始失眠了
那是多久以前的事？

25
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:37,840
這是在 2024 年 9 月
連續三個月

26
00:01:37,840 --> 00:01:41,200
我一天假都沒休過
週末加班，每天熬夜

27
00:01:41,200 --> 00:01:45,440
我當時心想：天啊，這會成功的
但我還不確定它現在是否好用

28
00:01:45,440 --> 00:01:49,360
因為它那時甚至還不太會寫程式

29
00:01:49,360 --> 00:01:53,440
回顧那段時間到現在
此時此刻最讓你驚訝的是什麼？

30
00:01:53,440 --> 00:01:57,840
不可思議的是，我們竟然還在使用終端機
這本該只是個起點

31
00:01:57,840 --> 00:02:02,120
沒想到這竟然也成了終點
第二點是，它竟然真的很有用

32
00:02:02,120 --> 00:02:05,720
在起步階段，它真的不太會寫程式
甚至是今年二月我們推出正式版（GA）時

33
00:02:05,720 --> 00:02:09,840
它寫出的程式碼大概只佔我的 10%
我那時並不真的靠它寫程式

34
00:02:09,840 --> 00:02:13,840
它在那方面並不擅長
大部分代碼我還是親手寫的

35
00:02:13,840 --> 00:02:17,400
所以，我們的賭注真的成功了
它在我們預期的方向上變得強大了

36
00:02:17,400 --> 00:02:22,880
雖然當時這一點並不明顯

37
00:02:22,880 --> 00:02:27,560
在 Anthropic，我們的思考方式是
我們不為今天的模型研發

38
00:02:27,560 --> 00:02:30,440
而是為六個月後的模型研發

39
00:02:30,440 --> 00:02:34,160
這也是我給所有
開發 LLM 應用的創辦人們的建議

40
00:02:34,160 --> 00:02:38,600
去思考那些目前模型
還做不太好的邊界在哪裡

41
00:02:38,600 --> 00:02:41,200
因為它總會變強的
你只需要等待

42
00:02:41,200 --> 00:02:45,400
回過頭來看，你還記得
最初靈感出現的時刻嗎？

43
00:02:45,400 --> 00:02:49,760
那是某個火花，還是
你腦中出現的第一個版本？

44
00:02:49,760 --> 00:02:53,880
有趣的是，這其實很偶然
它是就這樣自然演變成的

45
00:02:53,880 --> 00:02:57,200
在 Anthropic 團隊
長期以來，我們一直押注在程式開發上

46
00:02:57,200 --> 00:03:00,840
我們認為通往安全通用人工智慧
（Safe AGI）的路徑就是透過程式開發

47
00:03:00,840 --> 00:03:04,520
這一直是我們的核心理念

48
00:03:04,520 --> 00:03:08,240
實現的方式是：先教會模型寫程式
再教它使用工具，最後教它操控電腦

49
00:03:08,240 --> 00:03:13,280
你可以從我們在 Anthropic 建立的
第一個團隊 Anthropic Labs 看到這一點

50
00:03:13,280 --> 00:03:16,880
它催生了三個產品：Claude Code
MCP（模型上下文協議）和桌面版應用

51
00:03:16,880 --> 00:03:22,880
你可以看到這些產品是如何交織在一起的

52
00:03:22,880 --> 00:03:27,240
至於那個特定的產品
其實沒人要求我建立一個 CLI（命令行界面）

53
00:03:27,240 --> 00:03:31,440
我們當時知道是時候建立某種程式開發產品
因為模型似乎已經準備好了

54
00:03:31,440 --> 00:03:35,280
但那時還沒有人真正做出
能發揮這種能力的產品

55
00:03:35,280 --> 00:03:39,400
即便到現在，我們仍有這種
「產品趕不上模型能力」的感覺

56
00:03:39,400 --> 00:03:43,440
但當時的情況更瘋狂
因為那時真的沒人做出過

57
00:03:43,440 --> 00:03:47,560
於是我開始動手寫一些實驗性的代碼
我告訴自己：我們要建一個開發工具

58
00:03:47,560 --> 00:03:51,120
我首先要做什麼？
我得先搞清楚怎麼使用 API

59
00:03:51,120 --> 00:03:54,720
因為那時我甚至還沒用過
Anthropic 的 API

60
00:03:54,720 --> 00:03:58,680
所以我先寫了一個迷你的終端機應用
來呼叫 API，僅此而已

61
00:03:58,680 --> 00:04:02,800
那只是一個簡單的聊天應用
回想那時的人工智慧應用

62
00:04:02,800 --> 00:04:06,720
即便對現在不寫程式的人來說
大家最常使用的其實也只是聊天界面

63
00:04:06,720 --> 00:04:10,320
所以我就是從那裡開始的

64
00:04:10,320 --> 00:04:13,840
它就在一個終端機裡
我可以提問，它會給我回答

65
00:04:13,840 --> 00:04:17,320
後來「工具調用（Tool Use）」功能發布了
我純粹是想嘗試一下那個功能

66
00:04:17,320 --> 00:04:21,520
因為我那時還不完全理解那是什麼
我心想：這東西很酷，但真的有用嗎？

67
00:04:21,520 --> 00:04:25,120
可能沒用吧，總之先試試看

68
00:04:25,120 --> 00:04:29,200
Harj：你把它放在終端機裡，只是因為
那是讓它運作起來最簡單的方式嗎？

69
00:04:29,200 --> 00:04:32,840
是的，因為我不需要去寫用戶界面（UI）
當時只有我一個人在弄

70
00:04:32,840 --> 00:04:37,200
那時像 Cursor 或 Windsurf 這種
整合開發環境（IDE）正開始流行

71
00:04:37,200 --> 00:04:42,280
你有感到壓力，或者有人建議你
把它做成插件或功能完備的 IDE 嗎？

72
00:04:42,280 --> 00:04:46

00:00:27,160 --> 00:00:31,040

11
00:00:38,440 --> 00:00:43,120
模型本身就足以聰明到能自行理解？

12
00:00:43,120 --> 00:00:46,440
Boris：也許只要一個月。

13
00:00:46,440 --> 00:00:50,000
Boris：天啊。

00:01:00,000 --> 00:01:03,000

今天我們有一位非常特別的嘉賓

00:01:03,000 --> 00:01:07,000

Claude Code 的創作者與工程負責人

00:01:07,000 --> 00:01:10,000

三週睡不著覺的東西

19

20

人們是否也有同感？
大約在去年 11 月底

21
00:01:21,720 --> 00:01:25,320
我很多朋友都說，有些東西改變了

22
00:01:25,320 --> 00:01:28,320

當我剛創造出 Claude Code 時

23

雖然還不確定是否能成功

24

那是多久以前的事？

25
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:37,840
這是在 2024 年 9 月
連續三個月

而是為六個月後的模型研發

39
00:02:30,440 --> 00:02:34,160
這也是我給所有
開發 LLM 應用的創辦人們的建議

40
00:02:34,160 --> 00:02:38,600
去思考那些目前模型
還做不太好的邊界在哪裡

41
00:02:38,600 --> 00:02:41,200
因為它總會變強的
你只需要等待

42
00:02:41,200 --> 00:02:45,400
回過頭來看，你還記得
最初靈感出現的時刻嗎？

43
00:02:45,400 --> 00:02:49,760
那是某個火花，還是
你腦中出現的第一個版本？

44
00:02:49,760 --> 00:02:53,880
有趣的是，這其實很偶然
它是就這樣自然演變成的

45
00:02:53,880 --> 00:02:57,200
在 Anthropic 團隊
長期以來，我們一直押注在程式開發上

46
00:02:57,200 --> 00:03:00,840
我們認為通往安全通用人工智慧
（Safe AGI）的路徑就是透過程式開發

47
00:03:00,840 --> 00:03:04,520
這一直是我們的核心理念

48
00:03:04,520 --> 00:03:08,240
實現的方式是：先教會模型寫程式
再教它使用工具，最後教它操控電腦

49
00:03:08,240 --> 00:03:13,280
你可以從我們在 Anthropic 建立的
第一個團隊 Anthropic Labs 看到這一點

50
00:03:13,280 --> 00:03:16,880
它催生了三個產品：Claude Code
MCP（模型上下文協議）和桌面版應用

51
00:03:16,880 --> 00:03:22,880
你可以看到這些產品是如何交織在一起的

52
00:03:22,880 --> 00:03:27,240
至於那個特定的產品
其實沒人要求我建立一個 CLI（命令行界面）

53
00:03:27,240 --> 00:03:31,440
我們當時知道是時候建立某種程式開發產品
因為模型似乎已經準備好了

54
00:03:31,440 --> 00:03:35,280
但那時還沒有人真正做出
能發揮這種能力的產品

55
00:03:35,280 --> 00:03:39,400
即便到現在，我們仍有這種
「產品趕不上模型能力」的感覺

56
00:03:39,400 --> 00:03:43,440
但當時的情況更瘋狂
因為那時真的沒人做出過

57
00:03:43,440 --> 00:03:47,560
於是我開始動手寫一些實驗性的代碼
我告訴自己：我們要建一個開發工具

58
00:03:47,560 --> 00:03:51,120
我首先要做什麼？
我得先搞清楚怎麼使用 API

59
00:03:51,120 --> 00:03:54,720
因為那時我甚至還沒用過
Anthropic 的 API

60
00:03:54,720 --> 00:03:58,680
所以我先寫了一個迷你的終端機應用
來呼叫 API，僅此而已

61
00:03:58,680 --> 00:04:02,800
那只是一個簡單的聊天應用
回想那時的人工智慧應用

62
00:04:02,800 --> 00:04:06,720
即便對現在不寫程式的人來說
大家最常使用的其實也只是聊天界面

63
00:04:06,720 --> 00:04:10,320
所以我就是從那裡開始的

64
00:04:10,320 --> 00:04:13,840
它就在一個終端機裡
我可以提問，它會給我回答

65
00:04:13,840 --> 00:04:17,320
後來「工具調用（Tool Use）」功能發布了
我純粹是想嘗試一下那個功能

66
00:04:17,320 --> 00:04:21,520
因為我那時還不完全理解那是什麼
我心想：這東西很酷，但真的有用嗎？

67
00:04:21,520 --> 00:04:25,120
可能沒用吧，總之先試試看

68
00:04:25,120 --> 00:04:29,200
Harj：你把它放在終端機裡，只是因為
那是讓它運作起來最簡單的方式嗎？

69
00:04:29,200 --> 00:04:32,840
是的，因為我不需要去寫用戶界面（UI）
當時只有我一個人在弄

70
00:04:32,840 --> 00:04:37,200
那時像 Cursor 或 Windsurf 這種
整合開發環境（IDE）正開始流行

71
00:04:37,200 --> 00:04:42,280
你有感到壓力，或者有人建議你
把它做成插件或功能完備的 IDE 嗎？

72
00:04:42,280 --> 00:04:46
<command with shellId: 0 is still running after 60 seconds. The command is still running. Check the output above - if it looks like the command is waiting for input, use write_bash to provide it. Otherwise, use read_bash to continue waiting for output, or stop_bash to stop it.>

1
00:00:00,000 --> 00:00:03,800
在 Anthropic，我們的思考方式是
我們不為今天的模型進行開發

2
00:00:03,800 --> 00:00:06,440
而是為六個月後的模型做準備

3
00:00:06,440 --> 00:00:10,240
這其實仍然是我對
那些基於 LLM 開發的創辦人們的建議

4
00:00:10,240 --> 00:00:14,640
試著去思考，目前模型
還不太擅長的領域邊界在哪裡？

5
00:00:14,640 --> 00:00:18,520
因為它終將會變得擅長
Claude Code 的所有程式碼

6
00:00:18,520 --> 00:00:21,480
都經過了一次又一次的不斷重寫

7
00:00:21,480 --> 00:00:27,160
Claude Code 現在沒有任何部分
是六個月前留下來的

8
00:00:27,160 --> 00:00:31,040
你嘗試新事物、交給用戶
與用戶交流、並從中學習

9
00:00:31,040 --> 00:00:34,360
最終你可能會得到一個好點子
但也可能不會

10
00:00:34,360 --> 00:00:38,440
Harj：你是否也認為，或許六個月後
就不再需要這麼明確的提示詞了？

11
00:00:38,440 --> 00:00:43,120
模型本身就足以聰明到能自行理解？

12
00:00:43,120 --> 00:00:46,440
Boris：也許只要一個月。
Garry：一個月後就不需要計畫模式了？

13
00:00:46,440 --> 00:00:50,000
Boris：天啊。

14
00:00:50,000 --> 00:01:00,000
[LIGHTCONE PODCAST]

15
00:01:00,000 --> 00:01:03,000
歡迎收看新一集的 Lightcone
今天我們有一位非常特別的嘉賓

16
00:01:03,000 --> 00:01:07,000
Boris Cherny，他是
Claude Code 的創作者與工程負責人

17
00:01:07,000 --> 00:01:10,000
Boris，感謝你的加入
謝謝你們邀請我

18
00:01:10,000 --> 00:01:13,760
感謝你創造了一個讓我連續
三週睡不著覺的東西

19
00:01:13,760 --> 00:01:18,240
我對 Claude Code 非常著迷
它感覺就像是火箭助推器

20
00:01:18,240 --> 00:01:21,720
人們是否也有同感？
大約在去年 11 月底

21
00:01:21,720 --> 00:01:25,320
我很多朋友都說，有些東西改變了

22
00:01:25,320 --> 00:01:28,320
我記得對我來說
當我剛創造出 Claude Code 時

23
00:01:28,320 --> 00:01:31,680
雖然還不確定是否能成功
但我感覺自己捕捉到了什麼

24
00:01:31,680 --> 00:01:34,840
那時我也開始失眠了
那是多久以前的事？

25
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:37,840
這是在 2024 年 9 月
連續三個月

26
00:01:37,840 --> 00:01:41,200
我一天假都沒休過
週末加班，每天熬夜

27
00:01:41,200 --> 00:01:45,440
我當時心想：天啊，這會成功的
但我還不確定它現在是否好用

28
00:01:45,440 --> 00:01:49,360
因為它那時甚至還不太會寫程式

29
00:01:49,360 --> 00:01:53,440
回顧那段時間到現在
此時此刻最讓你驚訝的是什麼？

30
00:01:53,440 --> 00:01:57,840
不可思議的是，我們竟然還在使用終端機
這本該只是個起點

31
00:01:57,840 --> 00:02:02,120
沒想到這竟然也成了終點
第二點是，它竟然真的很有用

32
00:02:02,120 --> 00:02:05,720
在起步階段，它真的不太會寫程式
甚至是今年二月我們推出正式版（GA）時

33
00:02:05,720 --> 00:02:09,840
它寫出的程式碼大概只佔我的 10%
我那時並不真的靠它寫程式

34
00:02:09,840 --> 00:02:13,840
它在那方面並不擅長
大部分代碼我還是親手寫的

35
00:02:13,840 --> 00:02:17,400
所以，我們的賭注真的成功了
它在我們預期的方向上變得強大了

36
00:02:17,400 --> 00:02:22,880
雖然當時這一點並不明顯

41
00:02:38,600 --> 00:02:41,200
因為它總會變強的
你只需要等待

42
00:02:41,200 --> 00:02:45,400
回過頭來看，你還記得
最初靈感出現的時刻嗎？

43
00:02:45,400 --> 00:02:49,760
那是某個火花，還是
你腦中出現的第一個版本？

44
00:02:49,760 --> 00:02:53,880
有趣的是，這其實很偶然
它是就這樣自然演變成的

45
00:02:53,880 --> 00:02:57,200
在 Anthropic 團隊
長期以來，我們一直押注在程式開發上

46
00:02:57,200 --> 00:03:00,840
我們認為通往安全通用人工智慧
（Safe AGI）的路徑就是透過程式開發

47
00:03:00,840 --> 00:03:04,520
這一直是我們的核心理念

48
00:03:04,520 --> 00:03:08,240
實現的方式是：先教會模型寫程式
再教它使用工具，最後教它操控電腦

49
00:03:08,240 --> 00:03:13,280
你可以從我們在 Anthropic 建立的
第一個團隊 Anthropic Labs 看到這一點

50
00:03:13,280 --> 00:03:16,880
它催生了三個產品：Claude Code
MCP（模型上下文協議）和桌面版應用

51
00:03:16,880 --> 00:03:22,880
你可以看到這些產品是如何交織在一起的

52
00:03:22,880 --> 00:03:27,240
至於那個特定的產品
其實沒人要求我建立一個 CLI（命令行界面）

53
00:03:27,240 --> 00:03:31,440
我們當時知道是時候建立某種程式開發產品
因為模型似乎已經準備好了

54
00:03:31,440 --> 00:03:35,280
但那時還沒有人真正做出
能發揮這種能力的產品

55
00:03:35,280 --> 00:03:39,400
即便到現在，我們仍有這種
「產品趕不上模型能力」的感覺

56
00:03:39,400 --> 00:03:43,440
但當時的情況更瘋狂
因為那時真的沒人做出過

57
00:03:43,440 --> 00:03:47,560
於是我開始動手寫一些實驗性的代碼
我告訴自己：我們要建一個開發工具

58
00:03:47,560 --> 00:03:51,120
我首先要做什麼？
我得先搞清楚怎麼使用 API

59
00:03:51,120 --> 00:03:54,720
因為那時我甚至還沒用過
Anthropic 的 API

60
00:03:54,720 --> 00:03:58,680
所以我先寫了一個迷你的終端機應用
來呼叫 API，僅此而已

61
00:03:58,680 --> 00:04:02,800
那只是一個簡單的聊天應用
回想那時的人工智慧應用

62
00:04:02,800 --> 00:04:06,720
即便對現在不寫程式的人來說
大家最常使用的其實也只是聊天界面

63
00:04:06,720 --> 00:04:10,320
所以我就是從那裡開始的

64
00:04:10,320 --> 00:04:13,840
它就在一個終端機裡
我可以提問，它會給我回答

65
00:04:13,840 --> 00:04:17,320
後來「工具調用（Tool Use）」功能發布了
我純粹是想嘗試一下那個功能

66
00:04:17,320 --> 00:04:21,520
因為我那時還不完全理解那是什麼
我心想：這東西很酷，但真的有用嗎？

67
00:04:21,520 --> 00:04:25,120
可能沒用吧，總之先試試看

68
00:04:25,120 --> 00:04:29,200
Harj：你把它放在終端機裡，只是因為
那是讓它運作起來最簡單的方式嗎？

69
00:04:29,200 --> 00:04:32,840
是的，因為我不需要去寫用戶界面（UI）
當時只有我一個人在弄

70
00:04:32,840 --> 00:04:37,200
那時像 Cursor 或 Windsurf 這種
整合開發環境（IDE）正開始流行

71
00:04:37,200 --> 00:04:42,280
你有感到壓力，或者有人建議你
把它做成插件或功能完備的 IDE 嗎？

72
00:04:42,280 --> 00:04:46,280
當時完全沒有壓力
因為我們甚至不知道自己想做什麼

73
00:04:46,280 --> 00:04:49,760
團隊當時處於一種探索模式
我們隱約知道想在程式開發領域做點什麼

74
00:04:49,760 --> 00:04:54,360
但不確定具體是什麼
沒人有十足的把握，那是我的工作去弄清楚

75
00:04:54,360 --> 00:04:58,840
於是我給了模型一個 Bash 工具
那是第一個我給它的工具

76
00:04:58,840 --> 00:05:02,160
純粹是因為那是我們說明文件（Docs）
裡的現成範例

77
00:05:02,160 --> 00:05:06,440
我直接把 Python 的範例搬過來
改寫成 TypeScript，我就這樣寫出來了

78
00:05:06,440 --> 00:05:10,240
我那時不知道模型能用 Bash 做什麼
所以我試著叫它讀取文件

79
00:05:10,240 --> 00:05:13,320
它竟然能讀取，我就覺得「嘿，這很酷」

80
00:05:13,320 --> 00:05:17,200
接著我好奇它還能做什麼
我問它：「我現在正在聽什麼音樂？」

81
00:05:17,200 --> 00:05:21,080
它竟然寫了一段 AppleScript
去操控我的 Mac 電腦

82
00:05:21,080 --> 00:05:24,200
在音樂播放器裡查找我正在聽的歌
天啊

83
00:05:24,200 --> 00:05:28,480
而那只是 Claude 3.5 Sonnet 模型
我沒想過模型能做到那種程度

84
00:05:28,480 --> 00:05:32,840
那是我第一次真正感受到
通用人工智慧（AGI）時刻的降臨

85
00:05:32,840 --> 00:05:36,440
天啊，模型自己想要使用工具
這就是它想做的事

86
00:05:36,440 --> 00:05:41,200
這太令人著迷了
這是一個非主流的發現

87
00:05:41,200 --> 00:05:45,440
Claude Code 在這種簡潔優雅的
形態下運作得這麼出色

88
00:05:45,440 --> 00:05:48,320
雖然終端機已經存在很久了
但它似乎是個很好的設計約束

89
00:05:48,320 --> 00:05:52,640
它讓許多有趣的開發者體驗成真
那感覺不像是工作，而是樂趣

90
00:05:52,640 --> 00:05:57,480
作為一名開發者，我甚至不需要去
思考文件在哪裡或一切放在哪

91
00:05:57,480 --> 00:06:01,840
這一切的發現幾乎可以說是...
純屬意外？

92
00:06:01,840 --> 00:06:05,480
是的，完全是意外
我記得在終端機版本

93
00:06:05,480 --> 00:06:10,480
開始在公司內部流行之後
老實說，在做出第一個原型後的兩天

94
00:06:10,480 --> 00:06:14,320
我就開始把它交給我的團隊去內部試用
也就是所謂的「吃自家狗食（Dogfooding）」

95
00:06:14,320 --> 00:06:18,520
因為如果你有了一個點子
且它看起來很有用

96
00:06:18,520 --> 00:06:21,520
你第一件事就是想把它交給別人
看看他們會怎麼使用它

97
00:06:21,520 --> 00:06:25,560
隔天我來到公司
坐在我對面的另一位工程師 Robert

98
00:06:25,560 --> 00:06:29,480
他電腦上竟然已經跑著 Claude Code
並且正用它來寫程式

99
00:06:29,480 --> 00:06:33,760
我驚呼：「你在做什麼？
這東西還沒準備好，這只是個原型！」

100
00:06:33,760 --> 00:06:37,840
但在那種簡約的形態下，它已經很有用了

101
00:06:37,840 --> 00:06:41,600
我記得在我們進行內部發布審核
準備對外發布 Claude Code 時

102
00:06:41,600 --> 00:06:45,400
大概是在去年 12 月或 11 月

103
00:06:45,400 --> 00:06:49,760
Dario（Anthropic CEO）問我
他看內部使用數據表，曲線簡直是垂直上升

104
00:06:49,760 --> 00:06:54,200
他問我：「你有強迫工程師使用它嗎？
為什麼要規定他們一定要用？」

105
00:06:54,200 --> 00:06:58,040
我回答：「不，完全沒有
我只是貼了一篇相關文章，他們就開始推廣了」

106
00:06:58,040 --> 00:07:02,320
老實說，這真的是一場意外
我們從 CLI 開始，是因為它成本最低

107
00:07:02,320 --> 00:07:06,200
然後它就這樣維持了一段時間

108
00:07:06,200 --> 00:07:11,560
所以在 2024 年那個階段
工程師們是怎麼使用它的？

109
00:07:11,560 --> 00:07:15,760
那時已經在用它發布代碼了嗎？
還是以其他方式使用？

110
00:07:15,760 --> 00:07:19,200
那時模型在寫程式方面其實還不強
我個人是把它用在 Git 的自動化上

111
00:07:19,200 --> 00:07:23,880
我想現在我大概已經忘光了
大部分的 Git 命令了

112
00:07:23,880 --> 00:07:27,520
因為 Claude Code 幫我做了太久了
Bash 命令的自動化

113
00:07:27,520 --> 00:07:31,440
那是它非常早期的一個使用場景
還有像操作 Kubernetes 這種事

114
00:07:31,440 --> 00:07:34,880
那時確實已經有人開始用它寫程式
有些早期的跡象

115
00:07:34,880 --> 00:07:38,560
我想第一個場景其實是寫單元測試
因為風險較低，而模型那時其實還不夠好

116
00:07:38,560 --> 00:07:44,040
但大家都在嘗試弄清楚
如何駕馭這個工具

117
00:07:44,040 --> 00:07:47,760
我們觀察到的一件事是
大家開始為自己編寫 Markdown 文件

118
00:07:47,760 --> 00:07:51,120
然後讓模型去讀取那些 Markdown 文件

119
00:07:51,120 --> 00:07:55,720
這就是「CLAUDE.md」的由來
對我來說，單一最重要的產品原則

120
00:07:55,720 --> 00:07:59,600
就是「潛在需求（Latent Demand）」

121
00:07:59,600 --> 00:08:03,680
在這個最初的 CLI 版本之後
這個產品的每一點進步都源於潛在需求

122
00:08:03,680 --> 00:08:07,760
而 CLAUDE.md 就是一個很好的例子

123
00:08:07,760 --> 00:08:12,120
還有另一個有趣的通用原則
你可以針對模型本身進行開發

124
00:08:12,120 --> 00:08:16,120
或者在模型周圍搭建「腳手架（Scaffolding）」
來稍微提升性能

125
00:08:16,120 --> 00:08:20,320
根據不同領域，你可以提升
大約 10% 到 20% 的性能

126
00:08:20,320 --> 00:08:23,560
但基本上，這些增益在
下一個模型推出時就會被抹平

127
00:08:23,560 --> 00:08:27,520
所以你要麼現在搭建腳手架
獲得一點性能提升，然後以後再重做

128
00:08:27,520 --> 00:08:31,440
要麼就等下一個模型
那樣你就能免費獲得同樣的性能

129
00:08:31,440 --> 00:08:35,480
CLAUDE.md 和一些腳手架就是例子
這也是我們堅持使用 CLI 的原因

130
00:08:35,480 --> 00:08:39,400
因為我們覺得
目前沒有任何一個 UI 界面

131
00:08:39,400 --> 00:08:44,120
能在六個月後依然具有代表性
因為模型進步得實在太快了

132
00:08:44,120 --> 00:08:47,880
早些時候我們曾討論過
要不要比較一下大家的 CLAUDE.md

133
00:08:47,880 --> 00:08:51,560
但你說了一句非常有深度的話
你說你的文件其實非常短

134
00:08:51,560 --> 00:08:55,160
這跟大家的預期剛好相反
為什麼會這樣？你的文件裡寫了什麼？

135
00:08:55,160 --> 00:08:58,400
我來之前特別檢查了一下
我的 CLAUDE.md 只有兩件事

136
00:08:58,400 --> 00:09:03,040
而且只有兩行

137
00:09:03,040 --> 00:09:07,320
第一行是：每當我提交一個 PR（合併請求）
就啟用「自動合併（Auto Merge）」

138
00:09:07,320 --> 00:09:12,120
只要有人通過審核，就自動合併
這樣我就可以專心寫代碼

139
00:09:12,120 --> 00:09:15,320
不需要在代碼審核流程中來回折騰

140
00:09:15,320 --> 00:09:19,280
第二行是：每當我提交 PR
就發布到我們內部團隊的頻道

141
00:09:19,280 --> 00:09:23,280
這樣別人就能幫我蓋章通過
讓我能繼續前進

142
00:09:23,280 --> 00:09:27,600
除此之外，所有的指令都在
我們代碼庫共享的 CLAUDE.md 裡

143
00:09:27,600 --> 00:09:31,440
那是我們全團隊一週
會更新好幾次的共用文件

144
00:09:31,440 --> 00:09:35,760
我常會看到別人的 PR 裡
犯了些完全可以避免的錯誤

145
00:09:35,760 --> 00:09:40,240
我就會直接標註 Claude 說
「@Claude，把這個加進 CLAUDE.md」

146
00:09:40,240 --> 00:09:43,440
我一週
<command with shellId: 0 is still running after 60 seconds. The command is still running. Check the output above - if it looks like the command is waiting for input, use write_bash to provide it. Otherwise, use read_bash to continue waiting for output, or stop_bash to stop it.>

輸出格式：SRT
